children.name: Pluvialis
children.description: Large upright plovers of open habitats. The mournful whistle of the Golden Plover is the defining sound of Swedish upland mires and heaths; autumn flocks of tens of thousands gather on coastal farmland.

children.name: Pluvialis apricaria
children.description: Its plaintive whistling song carries across upland mires and bogs where it breeds in northern Sweden. Huge flocks gather on coastal farmland on migration, the spangled gold-and-black upperparts giving the bird its name.

children.name: Pluvialis squatarola
children.description: A larger, greyer cousin of the Golden Plover, breeding on high-Arctic tundra and passing through Sweden on migration. In flight it shows diagnostic black 'armpits' against white underwings.

children.name: Pluvialis fulva
children.description: Breeds on Siberian and Alaskan tundra and winters across the Pacific and southern Asia; slimmer and longer-legged than its European cousin, with remarkable long-distance migrations to oceanic islands.

children.name: Pluvialis dominica
children.description: Makes one of the longest migrations of any shorebird, breeding in Arctic North America and wintering on the pampas of southern South America, often flying non-stop over the Atlantic.

children.name: Oreopholus
children.description: A monotypic genus of high-altitude South American plover.

children.name: Oreopholus ruficollis
children.description: A handsome plover of the high Andes and Patagonian steppe, distinguished by a rich tawny throat and bold black belly patch.

children.name: Hoploxypterus
children.description: A monotypic South American genus, the pied plover is sometimes placed in Vanellus but differs in plumage and structure.

children.name: Hoploxypterus cayanus
children.description: A strikingly patterned black-and-white lapwing of South American river shores and sandbanks, with a chestnut patch on the breast and a delicate upright stance.

children.name: Phegornis
children.description: A monotypic Andean genus with a uniquely long, slender bill for probing among rocks.

children.name: Phegornis mitchellii
children.description: A rare and striking plover of high-altitude Andean bogs and streams, with a bold white-bordered black crown, slender curved bill, and barred underparts. Near Threatened due to habitat degradation.

children.name: Zonibyx
children.description: A monotypic genus for the rufous-chested dotterel of southern South America, placed here based on molecular phylogenetic evidence.

children.name: Zonibyx modestus
children.description: Breeds in southern Chile and Argentina and migrates north to winter in Bolivia and Peru; named for the rich chestnut breast band contrasting with a white throat.

children.name: Eudromias
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Eurasian Dotterel, separated from Charadrius on genetic grounds. The male alone incubates and cares for the young.

children.name: Eudromias morinellus
children.description: A confiding mountain plover that breeds on the high fell tops of Swedish Lapland, where the larger, brighter female courts the male and leaves him to incubate. On migration small 'trips' pause on traditional ploughed-field staging sites.

children.name: Charadrius
children.description: Core ringed-plover genus, now restricted to four species after the transfer of many species to Anarhynchus and Thinornis based on molecular phylogenetics.

children.name: Charadrius hiaticula
children.description: A neat, fast-running little plover of sandy and shingly shores, distinguished by a complete black breast-band and orange legs. Breeds on Swedish coasts and Baltic islands and uses a broken-wing display to lure predators from the nest.

children.name: Charadrius semipalmatus
children.description: The New World counterpart of the Ringed Plover, breeding across Arctic Canada and Alaska. Its partially webbed toes give it the name, and its single dark breast band is often narrower than in its Eurasian relative.

children.name: Charadrius vociferus
children.description: A noisy double-banded plover of North American fields and gravel; famous for its broken-wing distraction display to lure predators away from the nest.

children.name: Charadrius melodus
children.description: A pale, Near Threatened beach-nesting plover of North American shores and the Great Plains, the focus of major habitat-protection programmes.

children.name: Charadrius forbesi
children.description: A West African plover with a grey-brown back, white underparts and a narrow black breast band. Little known, it inhabits rocky hillsides and montane grassland in Guinea and Sierra Leone.

children.name: Charadrius tricollaris
children.description: A small African plover named for three narrow black bands across the white breast, with a distinctive red eye-ring. Common along rivers, lakeshores and estuaries throughout sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Charadrius placidus
children.description: A grey-brown Asian plover with a noticeably longer bill than its relatives, breeding along mountain streams in Manchuria and eastern Siberia. Winters to Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Charadrius dubius
children.description: A slighter, more freshwater-loving plover than the Ringed Plover, with a distinctive yellow eye-ring and no white wing-bar. It nests on gravel pits, riverbanks, and industrial ground across southern Sweden and much of Eurasia.

children.name: Charadrius collaris
children.description: A small, slim plover of sandy riverbanks and beaches from Mexico to Argentina, with a clean white underside, rufous crown, and a single narrow black breast-band. It is often found alongside other small plovers on exposed sandbars in major river systems. Unlike many relatives it is resident year-round across most of its range.

children.name: Thinornis
children.description: A genus of plovers expanded by molecular studies to include the little ringed plover and several Southern Hemisphere species formerly placed in Charadrius.

children.name: Thinornis cucullatus
children.description: A pale, sandy-coloured plover of southern Australian ocean beaches, named for the dark hood that covers its head and upper breast. Vulnerable due to disturbance and vehicle traffic on nesting beaches.

children.name: Thinornis novaeseelandiae
children.description: A boldly patterned black-and-white plover endemic to the Chatham Islands of New Zealand, with a distinctive white eye-stripe and orange-red bill base. Endangered, restricted to a few predator-free islands.

children.name: Elseyornis
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Black-fronted Dotterel, a small, boldly patterned plover of Australian and New Zealand watercourses with a distinctive black breast-band and chestnut back-patch.

children.name: Elseyornis melanops
children.description: A small, elegant plover of freshwater wetlands across Australia and New Zealand, with a distinctive black crown, white face, and chestnut breast band. Constantly bobs its tail while feeding.

children.name: Vanellus
children.description: Lapwings with broad rounded wings, elaborate crests and often facial wattles or carpal spurs. Found on every continent except Antarctica.

children.name: Vanellus vanellus
children.description: The tofsvipa, with its wispy crest, iridescent green back, and tumbling display flight, is a beloved harbinger of spring on Swedish farmland and wet meadows. Its plaintive 'pee-wit' call gives it many of its folk names.

children.name: Vanellus crassirostris
children.description: A distinctive lapwing of African swamps and marshes with exceptionally long toes that allow it to walk on floating vegetation. Its black cap, white face and red-and-black bill make it unmistakable.

children.name: Vanellus armatus
children.description: A striking black, white and grey lapwing of southern and eastern African wetlands, named for its metallic 'tink-tink' alarm call like a hammer on an anvil.

children.name: Vanellus spinosus
children.description: A boldly black-white-and-brown lapwing of African and Middle Eastern wetlands, armed with a sharp carpal spur on each wing used in defence of its nest.

children.name: Vanellus duvaucelii
children.description: A handsome lapwing of large Asian rivers from India to Indochina, with a black crown, chestnut belly, and prominent carpal spurs. Often seen in small groups on sandbanks and shingle bars.

children.name: Vanellus tectus
children.description: A small, striking lapwing of dry savanna and scrub in sub-Saharan Africa with a jet-black head, red bill base, and long white wattle. Its call is a sharp, penetrating 'kek-kek'.

children.name: Vanellus malabaricus
children.description: A dry-country lapwing of the Indian subcontinent, distinguished by bright yellow facial wattles and a black cap. Found on fallow fields, dry grasslands and stony ground.

children.name: Vanellus albiceps
children.description: A large, elegant lapwing of West and Central African rivers with a white crown, grey throat and long yellow wattles. Highly territorial on sandbanks and river islets.

children.name: Vanellus lugubris
children.description: A slim, grey-brown lapwing of West African savannas with a black forehead and white eyebrow. More subtle in pattern than other lapwings but easily identified by its mournful whistled call.

children.name: Vanellus melanopterus
children.description: A strikingly patterned lapwing of East and southern African highlands, with bold black-and-white wing markings visible in flight. The breast is grey, belly white, and crown black.

children.name: Vanellus coronatus
children.description: A common and adaptable lapwing of African grasslands and overgrazed land, named for the black cap encircled by a white halo.

children.name: Vanellus senegallus
children.description: A large, heavy-set lapwing of African wetlands and moist grasslands, with large yellow facial wattles and a distinctive white forehead. Its loud alarm call is a familiar sound across the continent.

children.name: Vanellus melanocephalus
children.description: An Ethiopian highland endemic, with a black cap, grey-brown back and white underparts heavily spotted with black. Inhabits montane grasslands and moorlands above 2000 m.

children.name: Vanellus superciliosus
children.description: A little-known migratory lapwing breeding in the Lake Chad basin, with a distinctive brown chest band separating the grey head from white belly. Winters southwards to the Congo basin.

children.name: Vanellus cinereus
children.description: A striking lapwing of East Asian wetlands with a soft grey head and neck contrasting with a white belly and brown back. Winters in Southeast Asia, often in small flocks on flooded fields.

children.name: Vanellus indicus
children.description: A familiar South Asian lapwing whose loud 'did-he-do-it' call rings across farmland and wetlands; readily nests on open ground including rooftops.

children.name: Vanellus macropterus
children.description: A large, long-winged lapwing endemic to Java, critically endangered and possibly extinct. Last reliably recorded in the 1940s, it inhabited marshes and river deltas.

children.name: Vanellus tricolor
children.description: A dry-country Australian lapwing with a black crown, white eye-stripe, and a broad black band across the breast. Unlike the Masked Lapwing it avoids coasts and is found on arid plains.

children.name: Vanellus miles
children.description: A large, noisy lapwing of Australia and New Zealand with conspicuous yellow facial wattles; famous for aggressively dive-bombing intruders near its ground nest.

children.name: Vanellus gregarius
children.description: A Critically Endangered lapwing breeding on the Central Asian steppes; populations crashed dramatically through the 20th century, prompting intensive international conservation efforts.

children.name: Vanellus leucurus
children.description: A slender, long-legged lapwing of Asian steppe lakes and wetlands, with a pure white tail and no black on the head. Often found in small flocks and readily identified in flight by its striking tail pattern.

children.name: Vanellus chilensis
children.description: The most widespread lapwing of South America and the national bird of Uruguay; a noisy, aggressive defender of its grassland and wetland nest sites.

children.name: Vanellus resplendens
children.description: A high-altitude lapwing of the Andean puna from Colombia to Chile, with iridescent green-glossed upperparts and a soft pinkish belly. Descends to lower valleys in winter.

children.name: Erythrogonys
children.description: A monotypic genus distinguished by its red legs and unique plumage pattern among Charadriidae.

children.name: Erythrogonys cinctus
children.description: A distinctive plover of Australian and southern New Guinean freshwater wetlands, identified at once by its bright red legs and bold black-and-white head pattern. Gregarious and often seen in small flocks.

children.name: Peltohyas
children.description: A monotypic genus superbly camouflaged for Australia's arid interior; formerly thought to be related to coursers.

children.name: Peltohyas australis
children.description: A uniquely adapted plover of the Australian outback, with cryptic sandy-buff plumage and a bold black V on the breast. It inhabits stony gibber deserts and claypans, and irrupts after rainfall.

children.name: Anarhynchus
children.description: A large genus of plovers greatly expanded after molecular studies confirmed that many Charadrius species are more closely related to the wrybill. Characterised by partial collars rather than full breast bands.

children.name: Anarhynchus asiaticus
children.description: A long-legged sand plover breeding on the dry steppes of Central Asia and wintering in eastern and southern Africa. The male has a rich chestnut breast band and a distinctive white forehead.

children.name: Anarhynchus veredus
children.description: A long-legged plover breeding on the dry grasslands of Mongolia and China and migrating to northern Australia, with a broad white eyebrow and deep chestnut breast in breeding plumage.

children.name: Anarhynchus atrifrons
children.description: Split from the Siberian Sand Plover complex; breeds on the high Tibetan Plateau and winters along Indian Ocean and Red Sea coasts. Distinguished by forehead patterning and mtDNA differences.

children.name: Anarhynchus mongolus
children.description: A small migratory sand plover breeding in the mountains and tundra of eastern Siberia, wintering on Indo-Pacific shores.

children.name: Anarhynchus leschenaultii
children.description: Breeds on Central Asian deserts and migrates to coasts of Africa, Asia and Australia; a long-billed sand plover of tidal flats.

children.name: Anarhynchus bicinctus
children.description: Breeds in New Zealand on braided riverbeds and uniquely migrates west to winter in Australia; marked by two contrasting breast bands.

children.name: Anarhynchus frontalis
children.description: A New Zealand endemic and the only bird in the world with a bill bent sideways (always to the right), used to sweep under riverbed stones for insect larvae; Near Threatened.

children.name: Anarhynchus obscurus
children.description: A stocky, rufous-breasted plover endemic to New Zealand; one subspecies is Critically Endangered with intensive nest protection underway.

children.name: Anarhynchus wilsonia
children.description: A stocky plover with a heavy black bill, found on sandy and muddy coasts of the Americas; specialises in catching fiddler crabs.

children.name: Anarhynchus collaris
children.description: A tiny, pale plover of sandy shores and river beaches from Mexico south through Central and South America. The narrow black collar gives it its name; otherwise very similar to the Snowy Plover.

children.name: Anarhynchus montanus
children.description: A plover of short-grass prairie and bare ground in western North America rather than shorelines; declining with the loss of native grassland and prairie dog colonies.

children.name: Anarhynchus alticola
children.description: A small, pale plover of high-altitude Andean lakes and salt flats, with a white forehead and a narrow black breast band. Its grey-brown back blends perfectly with the puna landscape.

children.name: Anarhynchus falklandicus
children.description: A handsome plover of southern South America and the Falkland Islands, named for two black bands across the white breast. Breeds on open short grass and migrates north along the coasts.

children.name: Anarhynchus thoracicus
children.description: A small, pale plover endemic to coastal Madagascar, with a narrow black breast band and a distinctive chestnut patch on the hindneck. Vulnerable due to habitat loss and disturbance.

children.name: Anarhynchus pecuarius
children.description: A small, well-camouflaged African plover that buries its eggs in sand when leaving the nest; widespread on inland and coastal flats.

children.name: Anarhynchus sanctaehelenae
children.description: A plover endemic to the remote South Atlantic island of Saint Helena, with sandy-brown upperparts, a white belly and a narrow dark breast band. Vulnerable and restricted to semi-desert scrub on the island's northeastern coast.

children.name: Anarhynchus ruficapillus
children.description: A common Australian plover of coastal beaches and inland salt lakes, named for the male's rich chestnut crown. Pairs are often seen running along the tide line in a delicate, rapid gait.

children.name: Anarhynchus nivosus
children.description: A pale, sand-coloured plover of the Americas, nesting on Pacific and Gulf Coast beaches and interior salt flats. The subject of intensive conservation management to protect its beach habitats from human disturbance.

children.name: Anarhynchus pallidus
children.description: A small, long-legged plover of East African coastal lagoons, salt pans and estuaries, with a distinctive broad chestnut band across the breast.

children.name: Anarhynchus peronii
children.description: A pale plover of Southeast Asian sandy beaches and mudflats, with a narrow black breast band and a white forehead. Near Threatened due to habitat loss and disturbance of its coastal breeding sites.

children.name: Anarhynchus marginatus
children.description: A common beach-nesting plover of sub-Saharan African coasts and waterways, with a white forehead and a pale sandy back that blends with the sand. It nests on sandy shores, riverbanks and lakeshores.

children.name: Anarhynchus javanicus
children.description: A small, pale plover of Indonesian and Malaysian sandy beaches and tidal flats, distinguished from the Malaysian Plover by its larger bill and darker crown. Vulnerable due to coastal development.

children.name: Anarhynchus alexandrinus
children.description: A pale sand-coloured plover of coastal beaches and saline lagoons across the Old World; declining in Europe due to disturbance of nesting beaches.

children.name: Anarhynchus dealbatus
children.description: A recently-split species from the Kentish Plover complex, found on the coasts of eastern and southern China and Southeast Asia. Distinguished by an all-white face and generally paler plumage.
